chunk_summary: This section discusses operational failures in bedside feeding for chronic cardiorespiratory disease, focusing on under-delivery of prescribed calories, causes, and recovery strategies to prevent cumulative deficits, especially in critical care settings.
learning_objectives: Understand common causes of under-delivery of prescribed calories in chronic cardiorespiratory disease patients.
Learn strategies to monitor and reconcile delivered versus prescribed calorie intake in hospital and home settings.
Develop missed-feed recovery plans tailored to individual patient needs and clinical conditions.
Recognize the importance of linking recovery plans to mechanism-based diagnoses, especially in high-risk patients.
key_facts: Under-delivery of calories is a common operational failure, often due to caregiver dilution, missed feeds, or reliance on low-calorie drinks.
Delivered intake <90% of the target for more than 24 hours should be treated as a system error requiring immediate correction.
Missed-feed recovery methods include adding a bolus, increasing caloric density in subsequent feeds, or using nocturnal continuous feeds.
Children with heart failure or aspiration risk cannot safely recover calories through extra volume or hurried oral feeding.
Cumulative calorie deficits, especially around surgery or intensive care, predict worse recovery outcomes.
reference_quote: Explicitly document which recovery method is permitted for that child, because children with heart failure or aspiration risk cannot safely 'make up' calories by extra volume or hurried oral feeding.
document_key_topics: Under-delivery of prescribed calories in chronic cardiorespiratory disease
Causes and prevention of feeding failures in hospital and home settings
Missed-feed recovery strategies and their clinical importance
Tailoring recovery plans to high-risk pediatric patients
